skill.name: longbridge-technical
skill.description: Provides Longbridge-focused technical analysis workflows for candlestick patterns, Ichimoku, RSI, MACD, Bollinger Bands, harmonic patterns, Elliott Wave, Chan Theory, Smart Money Concepts, and Turtle Trading signals.
skill.summary: Analyze market price action with Longbridge data and established technical analysis frameworks.
skill.author: longbridge

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many backtick and high-risk pattern matches, but most are false positives caused by Markdown inline code, fenced command examples, ticker text, and trading notation. Real risk remains because the skill instructs agents to run the Longbridge CLI, install Python packages, write temp files, and potentially fetch account-related data with user authorization. No evidence found of prompt injection, credential exfiltration, destructive commands, or confirmed malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Assistant-Directed External CLI and Package Execution
medium_findings.description: The skill tells the assistant to run Longbridge CLI commands, inspect command help, execute Python analysis snippets, and install third-party Python packages. This is legitimate for market analysis, but it can execute local commands and should require user consent before installation or account data access.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The instructions explicitly contain command examples and package installation guidance. The surrounding context is financial analysis, so the risk is operational rather than clearly malicious.

medium_findings.title: Market and Account Data Access Through Longbridge CLI
medium_findings.description: The Turtle Trading workflow lists commands for quotes, assets, positions, orders, watchlists, news, and filings. The file also says not to read positions without user authorization, but these commands can expose sensitive account and trading information if used without clear consent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The account-related commands are listed directly and the file includes a user-authorization guard. This confirms a real privacy-sensitive capability, but not malicious data collection.

medium_findings.title: Temporary File Use for CLI Output
medium_findings.description: The Elliott Wave reference instructs assistants to redirect CLI output to temp files and later pass a temp file to a Python script. This is a bounded workflow, but temp file handling can expose market or account data if files are reused or left readable.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The temp file paths and workflow are explicit. The content appears to be market-analysis data, so the risk is moderate and mitigable.

low_findings.title: Static Backtick Execution Findings Are Mostly Markdown False Positives
low_findings.description: The static analyzer interpreted many Markdown backticks as Ruby or shell backtick execution. Reviewed examples are inline command names, fenced Bash or Python examples, ticker formats, or output templates rather than executable Ruby code embedded in a program.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations are Markdown documentation and examples, not source code that automatically executes backtick expressions. Some commands are still intended to be run by the assistant, which is covered in medium findings.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography and Entropy Findings Lack Semantic Evidence
low_findings.description: The high weak-cryptography findings map to ticker examples, market names, indicator text, and output prose rather than cryptographic code. The high-entropy flag is consistent with a long multilingual Markdown reference and no binary or encrypted payload was found in the reviewed context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited sections found financial terminology and Markdown prose, not hash functions, ciphers, packed code, or encoded payloads. The conclusion is based on representative static locations, not every duplicate match.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found in Reviewed Files
low_findings.description: The reviewed files contain role, disclaimer, and output-format instructions for the technical analysis workflow, but no evidence found of instructions to bypass marketplace audit rules, claim pre-approval, ignore prior instructions, or override security review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious-looking text is limited to domain role guidance and disclaimer handling. It does not try to control the auditor or suppress security analysis.

dangerous_patterns.title: Shell Command Examples for Longbridge CLI
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes shell command examples that an agent may execute to fetch market, quote, and account-related data. These should run only after user approval and with clear symbol and account scope.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is explicit shell command guidance, including account-related Longbridge commands. Intent appears legitimate, but execution and privacy risks remain.

dangerous_patterns.title: Third-Party Dependency Installation Guidance
dangerous_patterns.description: Several references instruct users or agents to install Python packages before running analysis. Installing packages changes the local environment and may introduce supply-chain risk.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The package names and install commands are directly documented. No malicious package behavior is shown in the files, so the finding is about supply-chain exposure.

content.value_statement: Traders need consistent technical analysis across markets and indicators. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code through Longbridge data workflows and structured signal reports.
actual_capabilities: Fetches or requests OHLCV data through Longbridge CLI or Longbridge MCP workflows.
Explains candlestick, Ichimoku, RSI, MACD, EMA, Bollinger, ADX, OBV, and KDJ signals.
Supports harmonic, Elliott Wave, Chan Theory, Smart Money Concepts, and Turtle Trading analysis.
Produces multilingual reports in Simplified Chinese, Traditional Chinese, or English.
Includes error handling for missing CLI tools, missing packages, login issues, and insufficient data.
Adds risk disclaimers and asks for more data when a signal is not reliable.
limitations: It depends on Longbridge CLI, Longbridge MCP, or user-provided OHLCV data.
Some workflows require optional Python packages such as czsc or smartmoneyconcepts.
Technical signals are historical analysis and do not predict future returns.
Account-related Turtle Trading workflows require explicit user authorization.

use_cases.title: Daily Signal Review
use_cases.description: Review one stock with indicators, candlestick context, and a concise bullish, bearish, or neutral conclusion.

use_cases.title: Structured Wave Analysis
use_cases.description: Place a stock inside Elliott Wave or harmonic structures with Fibonacci reference zones and invalidation levels.

use_cases.title: Rule-Based Position Planning
use_cases.description: Apply Turtle Trading rules to calculate ATR, breakout status, unit size, stop levels, and add-on levels.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Indicator Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze AAPL.US with Longbridge daily data. Show RSI, MACD, Bollinger Bands, EMA trend, and a plain-language conclusion.

prompt_templates.title: Candlestick and Ichimoku Review
prompt_templates.prompt: Use Longbridge data to review 700.HK. Check recent candlestick patterns and Ichimoku cloud signals, then explain whether they agree.

prompt_templates.title: Wave and Harmonic Structure
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze TSLA.US for Elliott Wave position and possible harmonic patterns. Include Fibonacci zones, invalidation levels, and confidence notes.

prompt_templates.title: Turtle Trading Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Apply Turtle Trading rules to NVDA.US. Calculate breakout status, ATR N value, unit sizing inputs, stop level, and add-on levels.

output_examples.input: Analyze NVDA.US with standard technical indicators.
output_examples.output: A concise report with indicator values, individual bullish or bearish votes, a composite signal, data source notes, and an investment-risk disclaimer.

output_examples.input: Check whether 700.HK has a clear Elliott Wave setup.
output_examples.output: A structured wave report that names the likely stage, lists Fibonacci support and resistance zones, explains momentum confirmation, and states invalidation conditions.

output_examples.input: Run Turtle Trading analysis for AAPL.US.
output_examples.output: A rule-based trading signal summary with breakout channel status, ATR-derived risk levels, unit sizing guidance, and execution caveats.

best_practices: Confirm the symbol format and available Longbridge command flags before fetching data.
Use enough historical candles for the selected framework and refuse analysis when data is insufficient.
Treat all outputs as analytical references and include risk disclaimers for trading-related conclusions.
anti_patterns: Do not run account, position, or order commands without explicit user authorization.
Do not present technical signals as guaranteed investment outcomes.
Do not install optional Python packages without user approval and environment awareness.

faq.question: Does this skill place trades?
faq.answer: No. It analyzes technical signals and explains possible interpretations. It does not submit orders.

faq.question: Which markets does it support?
faq.answer: It is written for Longbridge workflows across Hong Kong, United States, A-share, and Singapore market symbols.

faq.question: Does it require Longbridge login?
faq.answer: Most price analysis can use public market data. Account-specific Turtle workflows require user authorization and a logged-in CLI.

faq.question: Can it work without the Longbridge CLI?
faq.answer: It can use a Longbridge MCP fallback when available, or analyze user-provided OHLCV data.

faq.question: Are the signals investment advice?
faq.answer: No. The reports are technical analysis references based on historical data and should include risk disclaimers.

faq.question: Why are optional Python packages mentioned?
faq.answer: Chan Theory and Smart Money Concepts workflows can use specialized libraries for better structure detection.
